ConfigBlock.status_buckets schema field declared; resolveStatusVocabulary merges hardcoded defaults with per-project config.status_buckets override; loadRoadmap consumes via Step 7. Description text above preserves the forensic record of the pre-closure state.

gaps.title: No render-time pagination primitive for projection rendering — large views overflow context windows
gaps.description: renderLensView, renderRoadmap (proposed), renderPlan (proposed), and any future projection renderer emit complete markdown with no pagination boundary. truncateHead (used by read-block via @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent) is intentionally NOT applied — full markdown is the contract; truncation defeats compositional projection. At small scales (≤10 phases per roadmap, ≤50 items per phase, ≤200 items per plan) this fits comfortably under typical context windows. At larger scales it overflows. The substrate currently has no primitive for: (a) page/section selection (render only phase X of roadmap Y), (b) summary-vs-detail rendering modes, (c) lazy materialization of nested compositions. Affects every aggregation primitive; particularly acute for the proposed roadmap/plan compositions.

evidence.reference: renderLensView emits all bins + items unconditionally; no pagination params; no summary mode.
read-block tool wraps payload in truncateHead per @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent — but lens-view rendering is exempt by design; no equivalent pagination knob exists for renderers.

gaps.impact: Today: not blocking — all current renders fit in context. Tomorrow: every roadmap/plan substrate consumer needs a way to address sub-views (one phase at a time, one bin at a time, summary-only, count-only). Without pagination primitives, the substrate's expressivity is bounded by the user's context window. Cannot be deferred indefinitely once roadmaps/plans land at scale; users will hit the wall on first large composition.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: render-time pagination; sub-view selection; summary vs detail rendering
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add optional render-time params to renderLensView/renderRoadmap/renderPlan: { detail: 'summary' | 'full', limit?: number, offset?: number, focus?: string[] (bin or item ids to render exclusively) }. SDK functions return paginated markdown with explicit cursor metadata (nextOffset, totalCount). Tool wrappers expose the params so LLMs can request specific sub-views. renderRoadmap's mermaid graph stays full (DAG topology rendering is cheap); per-phase item tables paginate. renderPlan's dependency graph stays full; item table paginates. Add a count-only mode that returns just the structural shape (phase names, item counts, status rollup) without item content. Filed forward per mandate-007 even though not currently triggered.

gaps.title: Decision lifecycle has no broadened-by state — superseded enum mis-fits enacted-then-broadened transitions
gaps.description: The decisions schema declares status enum [open, enacted, superseded] with the description characterizing superseded as withdrawn-without-enactment / not-a-real-decision. This covers open -> superseded cleanly but does not cover enacted -> superseded cleanly when the predecessor was a correct, ratified decision whose scope is later broadened by a successor decision (the case for DEC-0012 superseded by DEC-0013 on 2026-05-07). The schema forces this transition into the same superseded state with no way to distinguish retracted from broadened-by.

evidence.reference: status enum description
DEC-0006/0007/0010 (open->superseded, fits) vs DEC-0012 (enacted->superseded, semantic stretch)

gaps.impact: Substrate-level integrity ambiguity: a downstream reading status=superseded cannot distinguish retired-as-never-real from correctly-enacted-then-broadened. Lineage recoverable via supersedes/superseded_by fields but lifecycle vocabulary loses signal. Recurs as pi-context config-driven vocabulary matures (block_kinds, relation_types, status_buckets all evolve via broadening).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a fourth status enum value (extended or broadened) for enacted -> broadened-by-successor. Update description distinguishing superseded (retired without ratification) from extended (correctly enacted, scope broadened by successor, original preserved). Transition rules: superseded reachable from open; extended reachable from enacted. Couples to FGAP-006 (schema versioning).

gaps.title: Enum-vocabulary divergence across schemas — every schema declares its own per-field enums with no canonical registry, no shared identity, no display layer
gaps.description:
Across .project/schemas/*.schema.json (and packages/pi-project/registry/schemas/* mirrors), every block kind declares its own enum vocabulary for conceptually-shared dimensions. The pattern recurs at multiple field surfaces:

Priority/severity: domain [high,medium,low]; features [critical,major,minor,trivial]; framework-gaps [P0,P1,P2,P3]; issues [low,medium,high,critical]; spec-reviews [critical,major,minor,trivial]; state [low,medium,high,critical]. Five distinct vocabularies for the same dimension; no canonical mapping; no display-layer indirection.

Status/lifecycle: decisions [open,enacted,superseded]; features (multiple sub-fields) [proposed,approved,in-progress,in-review,complete,blocked,cancelled] / [todo,in-progress,in-review,done,blocked]; framework-gaps [identified,accepted,in-progress,closed,wontfix]; inventory [planned,in-progress,completed]; issues [open,resolved,deferred]; layer-plans [draft,proposed,decided,in-progress,complete,abandoned] / [pending,in-progress,complete,blocked]; phase [planned,in-progress,completed]; project [inception,planning,development,maintenance,complete]; requirements [proposed,accepted,deferred,implemented,verified]; research [planned,in-progress,complete,stale,superseded,revised]; spec-reviews [not-started,in-progress,complete,abandoned]; tasks [planned,in-progress,completed,blocked,cancelled]; verification [passed,failed,partial,skipped] / [passed,failed,skipped]. Fourteen-plus distinct lifecycle vocabularies. FGAP-013 captures one slice of this (cross-block aggregation requires normalization); FGAP-016 is the broader structural form.

Severity-level: audit / conformance-reference / conventions all use [error,warning,info] — a rare case of de-facto convergence with no enforcing registry; addition of a fourth schema would silently re-diverge.

Verification-method: audit [command,grep,inspect]; conformance-reference [grep,command,ast,inspect]; phase [command,inspect,human]; verification [command,inspect,test]; conventions [lint,test,review,manual]. Five vocabularies for what is conceptually method-of-checking.

Source/authorship: issues [human,agent,monitor,workflow]; requirements [human,agent,analysis]. Two vocabularies for what records-an-item.

Layer: features / framework-gaps / research all use [L1,L2,L3,L4,L5] — convergent by editorial discipline, not by registry.

The structural pattern: every enum is hardcoded in its schema file as a JSON Schema enum keyword, with no  to a shared fragment, no canonical identity layer, no display-name indirection. Renaming a value (e.g. priority high to urgent) requires editing every schema that uses it plus migrating data plus updating every consumer. The POC A identity-display split, the POC C two-phase token budget per status_buckets demo, and the POC E config.relation_types[] pattern all demonstrate the closure shape: opaque canonical ids in one config-driven registry per dimension, display labels per consumer, schema  to the registry rather than embedded enums.

evidence.reference: priority enum [low,medium,high,critical]
priority enum [P0,P1,P2,P3] — same dimension, parallel vocabulary, exposed during FGAP-015 filing 2026-05-07
severity enum [critical,major,minor,trivial] plus three distinct status enums for feature/story/task levels
status enum [open,enacted,superseded] (predecessor of FGAP-015 broadened-by gap)
status enum includes [stale,superseded,revised] — additional terminal states absent from sibling schemas
two distinct verification-result enums in same file ([passed,failed,partial,skipped] vs [passed,failed,skipped])
STATUS_VOCABULARY hardcoded TS table that normalizes a subset of these enums for cross-block rollup (FGAP-013 mitigation, not closure)
demonstrates the canonical-id / display-name split applied to block kinds; same shape generalizes to every enum dimension
demonstrates config-driven registry with category dispatch for relation_types; same pattern applies to priority_buckets / status_buckets / severity_levels / verification_methods / source_kinds

gaps.impact: Every cross-block aggregation, rollup, or projection must hand-normalize between vocabularies (or fail to compose). Every rename touches every schema-and-consumer that uses the value (display-name change requires data migration). New block kinds inherit the fragmentation pressure: a designer adding a new kind picks an enum vocabulary in isolation, which either silently diverges from siblings (most current cases) or only by editorial vigilance converges (the L1..L5 layer convergence is the rare positive case, achieved without any enforcing surface). Under pi-context the same authoring antipattern that produced the PLAN- prefix collision (issue-089) and the FGAP-013 STATUS_VOCABULARY hardcoding extends to every enum dimension — without a registry, every new block kind is a fresh opportunity for divergence. The lens reframe (PM is a lens upon memory) presumes vocabulary substrates; FGAP-016 names the structural barrier to that presumption.
gaps.proposed_resolution:
Introduce config-level vocabulary registries: config.priority_buckets[], config.status_buckets[] (FGAP-013), config.severity_levels[], config.verification_methods[], config.source_kinds[], config.layers[] (already adjacent in scope to layer-plans semantic). Each entry: canonical_id (opaque, immutable), display_name (mutable), description, category-or-ordering metadata where applicable. Schemas reference registries via $ref to a shared schema derived from config rather than embedded JSON-Schema enum literals. Schema-validator resolves the registry at validation time and emits per-registry-mismatch diagnostics. Rename-via-config-edit per POC A. Per-consumer display per POC C/E pattern. Couples to FGAP-006 (schema versioning / $ref composition / migration story) since this requires schema composition the current schemas do not use. Couples to FGAP-013 (the status-vocabulary slice). Couples to FGAP-011 (canonical schema-write surface, since registry edits today require direct fs Edit on schema files).

CLOSURE (agent/step-8.5-orchestrator 2026-05-09T23:58:13.827Z): Structurally closed by `899bcc3` (Step 4 6 shared enum schemas authored under packages/pi-context/schemas/ with $id pi-context://schemas/<name>) + `a4e8c9c` (Step 5.1 3 $ref consolidations: issues.source, audit.severity, conformance-reference.severity) — surface closed; residual reconciliation filed as FGAP-021. 6 shared enum schemas (priority/status/severity/source/layer/verification-method) authored at packages/pi-context/schemas/ with $id; 3 vocabulary alignments consolidated via $ref (issues.source, audit.severity, conformance-reference.severity); residual unreconciled vocabularies filed as FGAP-021. Description text above preserves the forensic record of the pre-closure state.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: enum schema composition; shared-vocabulary registry; canonical-identity-with-display-layer; JSON Schema $ref

gaps.title: Registry schemas lack $ref to shared enum schemas
gaps.description: 15 framework-shipped registry schemas (architecture, audit, conformance-reference, decisions, domain, handoff, issues, phase, plan, project, rationale, requirements, roadmap, tasks, verification) carry inline enum strings for fields like priority / status / severity rather than $ref-ing the 6 canonical enum schemas landed at Step 4 (FGAP-016 surface). Drift risk: an enum value added to status.schema.json doesn't propagate to consumers; per-schema enum drift was the original FGAP-016 forcing condition.

evidence.reference: Inline enum for priority / status / severity / source rather than $ref to the 6 shared enum schemas under packages/pi-context/schemas/
Inline enum for status; no $id/version per FGAP-006 discipline
6 canonical shared enum schemas exist with $id pi-context://schemas/<name>; registry schemas don't reference them

gaps.impact: Enum-value additions to shared schemas don't propagate to consumer block schemas. The cross-block consistency that FGAP-016 surface was filed to enable is structurally unrealized for the 15 framework-shipped registry schemas. User-customized .project/schemas/ overrides also can't inherit enum updates from shared schemas.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Per-schema migration adding $id (pi-context://schemas/<name>) + version: '1.0.0' + $ref substitution for inline enums where applicable. Migration registry entries (per Step 4's schema-migrations.ts) author 1.0.0->1.1.0 transitions for any enum-value renames. Non-blocking for Step 5 install/lens-view surface — registry schemas carry forward verbatim in Step 5; this gap closure is its own future step (5b or later) that links the closing commit via resolved_by.

gaps.title: upsertItemInBlock replacement-mode strips created_by/created_at on update path
gaps.description: upsertItemInBlock (Step 6 / commit 523fa96) implements 'find-or-append' with REPLACE semantics on the update branch — when an existing item with matching idField is found, the supplied item REPLACES it entirely. stampItem mode='update' (per Step 3.1 fix) deliberately does not refresh created_by/created_at. The interaction means: if a caller supplies an item without those fields and the schema declares them, the existing on-disk created_* values are stripped. Violates FGAP-004 attestation integrity (created_by should persist across updates so origin authorship is traceable).

evidence.reference: upsertItemInBlock body: on idField match, items[idx] = stampedItem (full replacement). stampedItem comes from maybeStampItem(..., 'update') which does not set created_*.

stampItem mode='update' branch: only modified_by/modified_at refresh; created_* explicitly skipped to preserve original-author intent — but only works when caller PRESERVES the field, not when caller REPLACES the whole item.
Phase A test #4 documents the actual behavior: upsert-update without explicit created_* in supplied item leaves those fields ABSENT on disk.

gaps.impact: Bundled monitor templates (fragility, work-quality) currently use merge: 'append' so no live regression. But any future consumer using merge: 'upsert' (with monitor or any other writer kind) silently destroys author attestation on every update. The FGAP-004 closure surface (Step 3) is undermined by Step 6's upsert primitive. Affects: monitor upsert-templates, any future agent/workflow using upsertItemInBlock for state mutation against a schema declaring created_*.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Two coherent shapes — user decides:  (a) Make upsertItemInBlock perform field-level merge instead of replace: on update, read existing item, shallow-merge supplied item over it (preserving fields not in supplied), then stamp. Preserves attestation by construction; matches typical 'upsert' semantic in databases.  (b) Make upsertItemInBlock preserve created_by/created_at specifically on update: read existing item, copy created_* fields into supplied item before replacement, then stamp. Surgical fix; preserves only attestation.  Path (a) is cleaner contract; path (b) is minimal change. Either closes the attestation-loss fragility. Migration registry entry not required (existing on-disk data unaffected; only future upsert calls change behavior).

gaps.title: No canonical validated write surface for monitor side-car state (instructions/patterns)
gaps.description: FGAP-011 (canonical schema-write surface, enacted Step 3 + Step 6) covers writes to .project/<block>.json only — block-api derives schema from .project/schemas/<block>.schema.json. Monitor side-car state files (per-monitor instructions and learned patterns) live in the discovery-tier dir (.pi/monitors/<name>/, ~/.pi/agent/monitors/<name>/, or bundled examples/<name>/) NOT under .project/. They are written via parallel ungated fs.writeFileSync paths at packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:947 (saveInstructions writing MonitorInstruction[]) and :1485 (pattern-learning writer writing MonitorPattern[]). Both bypass three of issue-065's four enumerated bypasses: AJV schema validation against their format schemas (packages/pi-behavior-monitors/schemas/{monitor,patterns}.schema.json declare the format but no validation runs at write time), proper-lockfile contention guard, centralized monitor-bypass detection. The fourth bypass (DispatchContext author stamping) doesn't apply because the side-car format schemas don't declare author fields.

evidence.reference: Line 947: saveInstructions writes MonitorInstruction[] via fs.writeFileSync to monitor.resolvedInstructionsPath (resolved at line 447 from the monitor spec's instructions.path field). No AJV validation, no lock.

Line 1485: pattern-learning writer writes MonitorPattern[] via fs.writeFileSync to monitor.resolvedPatternsPath (resolved at line 446 from the monitor spec's patterns.path field). No AJV validation, no lock.

Format schemas exist for monitor + patterns but are not consumed by the side-car write paths. Step 6's executeWriteAction refactor (commit 523fa96) closed equivalent bypasses for .project/ block writes via block-api routing; no analogous surface exists for non-.project/ side-car state.
block-api primitives (appendToBlock, updateItemInBlock, upsertItemInBlock, writeBlock) hardcode .project/<block>.json path resolution via blockFilePath. Cannot target arbitrary discovery-dir paths.

gaps.impact: Monitor side-car state writes can corrupt under concurrent access (no lock), can drift silently from their format schemas (no validation), and have no centralized choke point that recognizes 'side-car write happened here'. Compounds every time a monitor learns a new pattern (line 1485 fires on every NEW classify verdict) or the user adds a steering rule (line 947 fires on every /monitors rules add). FGAP-011 is partially enacted: substrate writes are validated; framework-level side-car writes are not.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend the canonical validated write surface to cover non-.project/ framework state. Two coherent shapes — user decides:  (a) Generalize block-api to take an arbitrary base path + schema path pair: appendToTypedFile(filePath, schemaPath, arrayKey, item). Existing block-api primitives become thin wrappers passing the .project/-derived paths. Monitor side-car writers call the new primitive with discovery-dir paths + framework schema paths.  (b) Add a parallel pi-behavior-monitors-internal validated write helper that wraps the existing fs.writeFileSync sites with proper-lockfile + AJV validation against the framework schemas, without involving block-api. Smaller surface, no block-api generalization, but creates a parallel ungated path elsewhere if other consumers need similar support.  Path (a) is the FGAP-011-extension story (one canonical surface for all validated writes); path (b) is the surgical fix (closes the immediate side-car bypass without architectural change). Either closes the three remaining bypasses for monitor side-car writes.

gaps.title: Find-or-merge block-api primitives lack typed-file generalization
gaps.description: FGAP-019 closure (commit 2160c63) generalized writeBlock and appendToBlock to typed-file primitives (writeTypedFile, appendToTypedFile) operating on arbitrary (filePath, schemaPath) pairs. The other six block-api primitives (updateItemInBlock, upsertItemInBlock, appendToNestedArray, updateNestedArrayItem, removeFromBlock, removeFromNestedArray) still hardcode .project/-block path resolution via blockFilePath/blockSchemaPath. Their refactor as wrappers (Phase C of Step 6.1) routes through writeBlock for the final write — which now goes through writeTypedFile — but the find-or-merge LOGIC stays hardcoded to .project/-targeting at the wrapper boundary. Asymmetric surface: typed-file primitives exist for whole-file write + array-append but not for find-or-merge / upsert / nested-append / nested-update / remove operations on non-.project/ paths.

evidence.reference: writeTypedFile + appendToTypedFile exported (lines 377, 442). updateItemInBlock, upsertItemInBlock, appendToNestedArray, updateNestedArrayItem, removeFromBlock, removeFromNestedArray exported but only as .project/-block-name primitives.

learnPattern uses dedup-then-append today (loadPatterns + check + appendToTypedFile). If the contract evolved to upsert-by-id (id-keyed pattern that should refresh learned_at on re-detection), no typed-file upsert primitive exists — would force re-implementing the find-or-merge logic outside block-api or revert to fs.writeFileSync.

gaps.impact: Future consumers of non-.project/ side-car state needing find-or-merge / upsert / nested-mutation semantics cannot reuse block-api's atomic critical-section + AJV + ctx-stamping infrastructure. Each such consumer either (a) re-implements the read-mutate-write loop with its own lock/validate/stamp logic — recreating FGAP-019, or (b) constructs a synthetic block name pointing outside .project/ — abuse of the wrapper boundary. The architectural symmetry FGAP-019 closure was meant to deliver (one canonical surface for ALL framework writes) is delivered for whole-file + simple-append only.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extract typed-file equivalents for the remaining six find-or-merge primitives: updateItemInTypedFile, upsertItemInTypedFile, appendToNestedTypedFile, updateNestedItemInTypedFile, removeFromTypedFile, removeFromNestedTypedFile. Each follows the same generalization pattern as Phase A of Step 6.1 — operate on (filePath, schemaPath, ...) pairs, withBlockLock wraps the read-mutate-write critical section, ctx-stamping via maybeStampItem (already schemaPath-keyed per Phase B). Existing .project/-targeting primitives become wrappers as in Phase C. ~+200 net lines; test surface ~+10 typed-file find-or-merge tests + wrapper-continuity smoke. No live regression — purely architectural-symmetry completion of Step 6.1.

gaps.title: Substrate enum vocabularies not reconciled between shared schemas and registry consumers
gaps.description: FGAP-017 closure (commit a4e8c9c) added $id + version to 15 registry schemas + $ref consolidated 3 aligned vocabularies (issues.source, audit.severity, conformance-reference.severity). The remaining inline enums diverge from the 6 shared enum schemas authored at Step 4. Per-dimension state: priority — shared uses p-prefix [p-low, p-medium, p-high, p-critical] while 5 registry schemas use [low, medium, high, critical] (no p-prefix); status — shared uses workflow vocabulary [todo, in_progress, blocked, complete, unknown] while 8+ registry schemas use issue-tracker / document-lifecycle vocabularies (open/resolved/deferred, draft/active/blocked, planned/in-progress/completed, etc.); severity — shared uses [info, warning, error] while domain.priority uses [high, medium, low] and verification-method has subset relationships; verification-method — shared has 6 values [command, grep, ast, inspect, human, test] while audit/conformance-reference/phase/verification each use 3-element subsets. Drift surface for the unreconciled cases: enum value added to a shared schema does NOT propagate to consumer schemas because they don't $ref it.

evidence.reference: priority enum [low, medium, high, critical] vs shared [p-low, p-medium, p-high, p-critical] — divergent prefix convention
status enum [open, resolved, deferred] vs shared [todo, in_progress, blocked, complete, unknown] — issue-tracker vs workflow semantic divergence
verification-method enum [command, grep, inspect] is a subset of shared [command, grep, ast, inspect, human, test] — can't $ref because consumer is more restrictive
priority enum [high, medium, low] — yet another priority scale; 3-value vs 4-value mismatch with both shared and other registry schemas

gaps.impact: No live regression — schemas validate as authored; existing on-disk block items conform to current vocabularies. Architectural debt: the 'one canonical enum vocabulary per dimension' intent of Step 4 / FGAP-016 is not realized. New consumers may inherit from any of multiple competing vocabularies. Future enum-value additions to shared schemas don't reach unaligned registry consumers; tooling treating shared schemas as authoritative may produce false validation results.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Per-dimension reconciliation decisions, each requiring a data-migration consideration (existing on-disk block items use current registry vocabulary): (1) priority — drop p-prefix from shared OR migrate registry to add prefix; (2) status — formally accept multi-vocabulary-per-dimension (workflow vs issue-tracker vs document-lifecycle as distinct shared schemas) OR collapse into one canonical form; (3) severity — align scales (3-value canonical OR 5-value canonical) across audit/domain/conformance-reference/shared; (4) verification-method — formalize subset-as-restriction via JSON Schema's $ref + constraints OR accept inline subsets as the consumer-restriction pattern. Each decision needs a migration registry entry per Step 4's schema-migrations.ts to bump schema version + transform existing data. The decision space is genuinely architectural (substrate vocabulary semantics), not mechanical.

gaps.title: renderLensView does not emit per-member subsections for composition lenses
gaps.description: renderLensView at packages/pi-context/src/lens-view.ts:100 detects composition via view.lens.kind === 'composition' and emits a header line stating 'composition over N member declaration(s)', but bin grouping uses view.grouped.get(bin) which projects items as a flat union. The composition's perItemOrigin map (populated by resolveComposition with member-source tags per item) is not consumed by the renderer. Output flattens membership; readers cannot see which member contributed which items.

evidence.reference: Line 100-130 renderLensView body: composition path emits target/relation_type/source header lines but bin iteration (lines 125-130) operates on view.grouped without member dimension. perItemOrigin from ResolvedComposition is unused in this function.
ResolvedComposition.perItemOrigin: Map<string, string> exists in the resolveComposition return type and tracks member-source per item id, but renderLensView does not access it.

gaps.impact: Composition lens output collapses provenance. Two composition-lens shapes that aggregate the same items from different members produce identical render output. Curation suggestions (buildCurationSuggestions consumer) lose member context. Diamond-DAG-style lenses where the same item appears via multiple members render with no indication of multi-membership.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend renderLensView to: (a) consume view.lensView.perItemOrigin or accept a ResolvedComposition reference alongside LoadedLensView for composition-kind lenses; (b) emit a per-member subsection (## Member: <member-id>) before the bin-grouped items contributed by that member; (c) annotate items appearing in multiple members with origin tags. Surfaced by adversarial audit of FGAP-012 closure (commit 3625758).

gaps.title: validateRelations does not surface composition lens cycles
gaps.description: validateRelations at packages/pi-context/src/project-context.ts:411 walks relations.json edges and emits edge_cycle_detected via DFS recursion-stack tracking on hierarchy relation_types. Composition lens cycles (lens A members → lens B; lens B members → lens A, expressed in config.json's lens.members[]) are caught only via resolveComposition's internal visited-set which throws composition_cycle_detected (project-context.ts:633) when someone actually invokes resolveComposition. validateRelations does not proactively walk lens.members[] composition graphs; composition cycles surface only on consumer dispatch, not on validation pass.

evidence.reference: validateRelations (line 411) emits 7 codes covering edge integrity (edge_unknown_relation_type, edge_dangling_parent, edge_dangling_child, edge_cycle_detected, etc.). Code list does not include any lens-composition cycle code.

resolveComposition (line ~600) detects sub-lens cycles via internal visited-set + throws composition_cycle_detected. Throw reaches consumers of resolveComposition (loadLensView, lens-view consumers) but does not register as a validateRelations diagnostic.

gaps.impact: validateProject + project-validate-relations pi tool report 'clean' on a config with cyclic composition lenses. The cycle surfaces only when a downstream consumer (e.g. /project view <cycling-lens>) invokes loadLensView and resolveComposition throws. Pre-flight validation is not pre-flight for composition integrity.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend validateRelations (or add a sibling validateLensComposition function dispatched via lens-validator registry per Step 7's lens-validator dispatch) that walks every config.lenses[] entry with kind='composition', resolves member graph via the resolveComposition cycle-detection path, and emits a new SubstrateValidationIssue code (e.g. 'lens_composition_cycle_detected') when a cycle is found. Issue carries the cyclic lens ids. Surfaced by adversarial audit of FGAP-012 closure (commit 3625758).

gaps.title: No write-authority enforcement layer beyond schema validation
gaps.description: FGAP-004 closure landed DispatchContext + WriterIdentity stamping (writer attribution on every block-api write). The stamping records WHO wrote; it does not enforce WHO IS ALLOWED to write. block-api's withBlockLock + AJV schema validation gate WHAT shape is written, not WHICH writer kind is permitted to write to which block. A monitor with kind=monitor can write to .project/decisions.json (a human-authored block) and the write succeeds as long as schema validates; only the created_by stamp differentiates.

evidence.reference: writeBlock, appendToBlock, updateItemInBlock, upsertItemInBlock all accept optional ctx?: DispatchContext but do not consult any authority configuration. ctx.writer is read for stamping; not read for permission.
WriterIdentity union covers 4 kinds (human, agent, monitor, workflow). No corresponding allowlist/denylist surface exists in pi-context for which kind can write to which block name.
No registry schema declares an x-allowed-writers / x-write-authority extension; no config.json field declares per-block writer permissions.

gaps.impact: Monitor write-action routing (Step 6 / commit 523fa96) closed the bypass-the-validation-layer concern but not the bypass-the-intended-author concern. A misconfigured monitor.json action.write.block can target .project/decisions.json and append entries that pass schema validation; the only differentiator is created_by stamp on the entry. Cross-writer authority drift is undetectable at write time.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Define authority-matrix surface — either (a) per-schema x-allowed-writers extension declaring permitted WriterIdentity.kind values; (b) config.json write_authority object mapping block-name → allowed-kinds[]; or (c) WriterIdentity-aware predicate at block-api write surface that consults a registered authority-policy module (parallel to lens-validator dispatch). On violation, throw before write reaches AJV. Surfaced by adversarial audit of FGAP-004 closure (commit 001d11a + 2ed2102 + d24769a).

gaps.title: No cross-block status-vocabulary validator surfaces unknown status values
gaps.description: Step 7's roadmap-plan validateRoadmaps emits roadmap_status_unknown_value when a roadmap.status field carries a value outside ROADMAP_STATUS_ENUM. This single-block coverage exists only for roadmap. Other blocks with status fields (issues, decisions, tasks, requirements, plan, project, features, layer-plans, etc.) rely entirely on AJV schema-enum validation at write time. validateProject + project-validate pi tool do not surface 'this on-disk item carries a status value not in the current schema enum' diagnostics for those blocks. Schema migrations (FGAP-006 surface) that change enum values silently invalidate on-disk data until the next write attempt against that item.

evidence.reference: validateRoadmaps emits 7 diagnostic codes including roadmap_status_unknown_value, scoped to roadmap blocks only. No equivalent validator exists for issues.status, decisions.status, tasks.status, etc.

validateProject iterates getLensValidators() (Step 7's lens-validator dispatch) but no cross-block status-vocabulary validator is registered. The lens-validator dispatch surface accommodates such a validator but none is authored.

11 registry schemas declare status enums (verified via grep). A schema-enum mutation through writeSchema/updateSchema (Step 3 surface) does not retroactively diagnose pre-existing on-disk items whose status falls outside the new enum.

gaps.impact: Schema enum evolution silently strands on-disk data. Status values that became invalid via a schema migration remain readable but are undiagnosed until an LLM or workflow attempts to mutate the item. Cross-block lens rendering (groupByLens, lens-view consumers) may bin orphaned-status items into the (uncategorized) bucket without any signal that the underlying status value is schema-invalid rather than schema-omitted.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Author a lens-validator-dispatch-registered cross-block status-vocabulary validator (parallel to the roadmap-plan lens-validator pattern from Step 7): for every block with a 'status' property in its schema, walk all items and emit a SubstrateValidationIssue code (e.g. 'block_status_unknown_value') when an item's status falls outside the schema's declared enum at validation time. Couples to FGAP-013 (config.status_buckets vocabulary registry) and FGAP-006 (schema-versioning + migration registry). Surfaced by adversarial audit of FGAP-013 closure (commit d4ec3ce + 5fe8969).

gaps.title: Substrate bootstrap hardcodes .project/ — config-driven-from-line-1 claim unrealized at bootstrap edge
gaps.description:
Step 2 substrate SDK port (commit d4ec3ce) commit message claims 'config-driven vocabulary registries from line 1'. ConfigBlock declares a `root` field; getProjectContext / loadConfig / lens-view / roadmap-plan all consume config.root for substrate operations. But the bootstrap edge — WHERE pi-context looks for config.json before it can read config.root — is hardcoded to `<cwd>/.project/config.json` via PROJECT_DIR + SCHEMAS_DIR constants in packages/pi-context/src/project-dir.ts. Every block-api primitive composes blockFilePath(cwd, blockName) = path.join(cwd, '.project', `${blockName}.json`); blockSchemaPath similarly. config.root is read AFTER bootstrap; it cannot relocate the bootstrap dir itself. Result: user cannot choose the substrate dir name. The 'config is the source of truth' substrate principle is structurally undelivered — config.root only relocates substrate state INSIDE the hardcoded `.project/` location, not the location itself.

Secondary: subcommand surface is `/project init`, `/project install`, `/project view`, etc. — package was renamed pi-project → pi-context at Step 1 but command surface kept the old `/project` namespace. The command name implies `.project/` is the dir; the package implies `/context`. Both contradict config.root being the source of truth.

LLM rationalization in HANDOFF.md called this a 'Bootstrap exemption: config.json + relations.json continue at <cwd>/.project/' — that framing is LLM hedging, not a design decision. The exemption was never authored by user direction; it post-hoc justifies an incomplete fix as if it were deliberate.

DEC-0015 (enacted 2026-05-10) anchors the underlying principle ('config drives substrate location; no hardcoded substrate-dir paths anywhere') as permanent substrate canon. FGAP-026 is the current-state implementation-gap record for DEC-0015 — closes (status:closed) when the resolver + cascades + bootstrap pointer schema + /context init + migration ceremony land. The DEC-0015 principle remains permanent after FGAP-026 closes; future tool/primitive additions are checked against DEC-0015 even with FGAP-026 in closed state. Reopening DEC-0015 violations gets filed as new FGAPs (regression record), not by reopening FGAP-026.

evidence.reference: PROJECT_DIR = '.project' + SCHEMAS_DIR = 'schemas' constants. Single point where the substrate dir name is hardcoded.
blockFilePath(cwd, blockName) and blockSchemaPath(cwd, blockName) compose against PROJECT_DIR — every block-api primitive (16 of them post-Step-6.3) inherits the hardcode via these helpers.
PROJECT_SUBCOMMANDS register under '/project' namespace (init, install, view, lens-curate, roadmap-list/view/validate). Package renamed to pi-context at Step 1 but command surface kept '/project'.
ConfigBlock.root field declared but loadConfig itself reads from path.join(cwd, PROJECT_DIR, 'config.json') — root field can describe the substrate dir but cannot relocate WHERE config is found.
HANDOFF text 'Bootstrap exemption: config.json + relations.json continue at <cwd>/.project/' frames the hardcode as design when it is LLM rationalization of unaddressed work.

gaps.impact: Substrate-arc thesis structurally undelivered at the bootstrap edge. User cannot pick a substrate dir name; config.root indirection is cosmetic (relocates blocks within a hardcoded outer dir). Onboarding UX for new users (or LLMs) requires accepting `.project/` regardless of project conventions. Other consumers (pi-jit-agents, pi-workflows, pi-behavior-monitors) inherit the hardcode via block-api imports. Multi-substrate scenarios (a repo wanting two parallel substrates) impossible. The 'config-driven from line 1' commit-message claim creates a credibility gap with the actual code.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Replace PROJECT_DIR constant with per-cwd `resolveContextDir(cwd): string` reading a JSON-typed bootstrap pointer at `<cwd>/.pi-context.json` (carries the user-chosen substrate dir name; schema authored under packages/pi-context/schemas/bootstrap.schema.json with $id pi-context://schemas/bootstrap + version 1.0.0 per FGAP-006 discipline). Update blockFilePath / blockSchemaPath in block-api.ts to compose against the resolver. Update loadConfig in project-context.ts to find config.json at `<cwd>/<resolveContextDir(cwd)>/config.json`. Cascade through pi-jit-agents / pi-workflows / pi-behavior-monitors path-construction sites. Rename `/project` subcommand surface to `/context`. Rename `project-*` pi tool names to `context-*` (full rename; no aliases — rebuild branch unpublished, no downstream consumer). New `/context init` subcommand: prompt-required for the dir name (defaulting reintroduces the hardcode-dressed-as-default pattern that FGAP-026 itself calls out); creates `<dir-name>/config.json` with root: '<dir-name>' AND writes `<cwd>/.pi-context.json` containing the dir name. Migration ceremony for existing `.project/`-using repos: `pi context migrate` renames `.project/` → `.context/` (or to user-chosen name) AND writes `<cwd>/.pi-context.json` containing the new name. The four 'open design questions' the original proposed_resolution surfaced (tool-name rename scope, bootstrap pointer format, migration default, init UX when no arg) were LLM hedging — none represent real design space. Each has a single mandate-compliant answer per the architectural premise. Corrected proposed_resolution embeds the answers directly.

gaps.title: Framework cannot detect user-authored hardcoded substrate-dir references in block content
gaps.description:
DEC-0015 establishes config-driven substrate location with framework-side mutability via context-migrate (rename dir + update .pi-context.json + update config.root atomically). Framework code is hardcode-free by construction; rename propagates cleanly through the resolver. But user-authored block content (decisions, rationale, research, issues, etc.) is free-form text/JSON and MAY contain hardcoded substrate-dir path references in description / proposed_resolution / external_refs / impact / commentary fields. After a context-migrate rename (e.g. .project/ → my-substrate/), prose like 'see .project/decisions.json line 47 for X' goes stale. Framework cannot scan, detect, or auto-rewrite these references because they are user-content-shaped, not framework-data-shaped.

This mirrors the .project/ block-content staleness pattern Step 8.5 addressed by mass updateItemInBlock substitutions across 18+39 path sites — those substitutions worked because the ORIGINATING references followed predictable patterns (evidence[].file fields with packages/pi-project/ prefix). Free-form prose references (paragraph text mentioning paths) are not predictably-shaped and cannot be safely auto-rewritten.

Dual-nature dichotomy formalized: framework-side substrate location is config-mutable (DEC-0015 enforces no hardcoding); user-content-side staleness on rename is forensic-of-when-written (per feedback_rename_scope_discipline.md historical-by-design vs missed-rename distinction). User responsibility — convention recommendation: avoid hardcoding substrate-dir names in block prose; prefer relative phrases ('see decisions block entry for X', 'see project-edges-for-lens output').

evidence.reference: Step 8.5 closure (commit fff1495) preserved 2 packages/pi-project/ substring references in FGAP-012 + FGAP-016 description prose as forensic-of-pre-closure-state — those references are stale-by-design. After a context-migrate rename, all current substrate-dir references in block prose become similarly stale-by-design (or stale-as-defect depending on user convention).

block-api primitives expose updateItemInBlock with predicate-based field-level rewrites — but framework cannot construct the predicate for free-form prose path references (no field-level structure to target). Mass-rewrite via this primitive only works when references live in structured fields like evidence[].file.

DEC-0015 framework-side mutability assumption + DEC-0014 harness-confined main LLM together imply: agents under harness confinement cannot grep/sed user-content prose to update path references after rename. context-migrate's body must NOT attempt prose rewrites; rewrites would corrupt forensic-by-default block content.

gaps.impact: Post-rename (context-migrate from old-dir-name to new-dir-name), block prose containing path references to the old name displays as stale to readers. No live regression — substrate operations succeed; framework code resolves through pointer; reads/writes work. But human-readable block content (decisions explaining 'see .project/ for X', issues describing path-bound state, research entries citing file:line references) loses temporal accuracy of the prose. Documentation discipline mitigates: convention to avoid hardcoded substrate-dir names in prose. Framework-side: documentation must SURFACE the user-content-staleness caveat so users know to prefer relative phrasing.
gaps.proposed_resolution: P3 — accept the dual-nature dichotomy as substrate canon (framework config-mutable; user-content prose staleness on rename is forensic). Framework-side enforcement: documentation discipline per DEC-0015 consequence — surface the caveat in code comments at resolver/init/migrate, CLAUDE.md project conventions, root + per-package READMEs, skill-narratives (auto-loaded into LLM substrate consumers). User-side convention: prefer relative phrasing in block prose ('see decisions block entry X', 'via project-edges-for-lens', etc.) over hardcoded paths. Optional future tooling (NOT in scope for FGAP-027 closure): a context-rename-prose-scan tool that surfaces (does NOT auto-rewrite) likely-stale path references in block prose for user review — explicit human-in-the-loop, never automatic. The documentation surface MUST land regardless; the scan tool is opt-in safety net.

gaps.title: Substrate completeness measured by harness-confined-LLM arc-dispatch capability, not by isolated tool/primitive existence
gaps.description:
Substrate completeness has been measured arc-step-by-arc-step ('Step 1 lands; Step 2 lands; tools added; tests pass') — isolated tool/primitive existence as the proxy for completion. Under DEC-0014 (harness-confined main LLM as canonical execution surface) the actual completion criterion is 'could a harness-confined main LLM dispatch a representative arc-of-work to completion using only extension tools + JIT-agent dispatch via workflows, with no bash/read/write fallback?'. Today the answer is no across the rebuild arc — 22+ tool gaps enumerated in analysis/2026-05-10-tool-surface-gap-audit.md (filter-block-items, resolve-items-by-id, read-config, read-schema, workflow-execute, etc.); workflow-execute likely subcommand-only (per the audit's subcommand-only callout); arc-tracking blocks (roadmap/phase/tasks/relations/config) have no concrete instances in this repo. Without dogfood-completeness as the explicit completion criterion, sub-arcs that pass build/check/test individually still leave the substrate structurally inoperable for the consumer the substrate is built for.

The distinction: 'tool exists' vs 'tool composes into an executable arc'. Step 6.1 (typed-file primitives, FGAP-019 closure) lands 8 functions in JS source — tool-existence test passes. Step 6.3 (find-or-merge typed-file primitives, FGAP-020 closure) lands 6 more — tool-existence test passes. But under DEC-0014 lens, NONE of the 8 typed-file primitives have agent-reachable tool wrappers; the architectural-symmetry deliverable that closed FGAP-020 is invisible to the harness-confined main LLM. Tool-existence-as-completion-proxy hides this from build/check/test gates.

Meta-architectural framing surfaced during dogfooding-perspective discussion 2026-05-10 + load-bearing under DEC-0014 because the consumer the substrate is built for cannot validate substrate operability via build/check/test — only by attempting actual arc dispatch through the substrate's surface.

evidence.reference: 22 enumerated gaps + meta-pattern (pi tool surface systematically lags JS export surface; ~40% query coverage). Under DEC-0014 every gap becomes load-bearing because no bash/read/write fallback exists for the main LLM.

DEC-0014 (enacted 2026-05-10) establishes harness-confined main LLM as canonical execution surface. Implies the substrate's completion criterion is operability under that constraint, not isolated tool/primitive existence.

8 typed-file primitives from FGAP-019/020 closure are JS exports with no agent-reachable tool wrappers. Tool-existence completion proxy treated FGAP-020 as closed; under DEC-0014 lens the architectural-symmetry deliverable is invisible to the consumer.

Subcommand-only surfaces (/project view, /project lens-curate, /project install, /project add-work) per the gap audit are unreachable by the harness-confined main LLM under DEC-0014 — the build/check/test gate at Step 8 closure passed without surfacing this incompleteness.

FGAP-026 (config-driven bootstrap), FGAP-024 (write-authority enforcement), FGAP-005 (state-machine transitions), FGAP-002/003/007/008/009/010/014/015 (other identified gaps) all surface or amplify under the dogfood-completeness criterion. Without FGAP-028's framing, each is enumerated in isolation; under FGAP-028, they collectively define what 'arc-dispatchable substrate' means.

gaps.impact: Without dogfood-completeness as the criterion, substrate-arc work can ship as 'complete' while structurally inoperable for the consumer the substrate is built for. Concrete near-term consequence: v0.25.0 release (Task #12 / Step 10) could ship with all currently-enumerated FGAPs closed, all 4 packages tests green, all docs aligned, and a fresh-repo harness-confined main LLM still cannot author + execute its own arc because (a) filter-block-items doesn't exist (90 issues, can't query); (b) workflow-execute is subcommand-only (can't dispatch out-of-substrate work); (c) arc-tracking blocks (roadmap/phase/tasks/relations/config) have no concrete instances; (d) per-item read returns location not payload; etc. Step 9 (adversarial audit) and Step 10 (release) currently lack the dogfood-dispatch gate that would catch this. The rebuild arc could declare itself complete and ship a v0.25.0 npm publish that immediately surfaces as inoperable on first user dispatch.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add dogfood-completeness as the explicit substrate-arc completion criterion alongside build/check/test/skills/commit/release sequence in CLAUDE.md Completion Sequence. Operationalize: every arc-completion release runs a 'harness-confined dispatch dry-run' — synthetic main-LLM-as-tool-only invocation of a representative arc (e.g., file a new FGAP via append-block-item with DispatchContext, transition its status via update-block-item, dispatch a workflow that exercises a JIT-agent build, mark phase complete via update on roadmap/phase block) — using only extension tools, no bash/read/write fallback. If the dry-run fails because of missing tools/primitives, those become P0 closure items before release. Step 9 (adversarial audit, Task #11) gains a dogfood-dispatch test gate as part of its protocol — fresh-context audit subagent dispatched with HARNESS-CONFINED tool surface, asked to perform representative arc work, surfaces gaps as findings. Step 10 (release, Task #12) is conditioned on Step 9's dogfood-dispatch passing. Future arcs scoped against dogfood-completeness as precondition. Couples to FGAP-026 (resolver + bootstrap pointer enables harness-confined operability), tool-surface gap audit (concrete tool deliverables block dispatch capability), DEC-0014 (defines the canonical execution surface dogfood measures against). The 7-phase FGAP-026 closure plan needs re-sequencing under this criterion: high-impact tool gaps (filter-block-items, read-config, read-schema, workflow-execute) pulled INTO Phase 1 so subsequent phases become harness-confined-dispatchable; arc-tracking blocks (roadmap/phase/tasks/relations/config) authored mid-arc as both the dogfood-dispatch substrate AND the arc-tracking record. Per mandate-007 surfaced as substrate-canon, not architectural-debt-deferred.

gaps.title: Bidirectional scoped traversal primitive missing — walk-ancestors + walk-by-relation tools
gaps.description: DEC-0017 (work-unit context composed at dispatch time) requires bidirectional traversal of closure-table edges per relation_type. Today only project-walk-descendants exists; reverse traversal is not exposed. find-references (cross-block reverse-index) gap from the tool-surface audit overlaps this. The gather-execution-context primitive (FGAP-031) cannot exist without bidirectional traversal as foundational layer.

evidence.reference: walkDescendants exists; no walkAncestors. Reverse traversal logic is per-relation_type DFS over relations.json edges; primitive shape is symmetric to walkDescendants but reverse-direction.
8-rank consolidated impact ranking position 5: project-walk-ancestors. Position 10: find-references (cross-block reverse-index). Both load-bearing for DEC-0017 work-unit context composition.

gaps.impact: Without bidirectional traversal, work-unit context cannot be gathered — backward edges from a unit to its constraining/motivating context are invisible. Under DEC-0014/0016 dogfood-completeness lens, work-unit dispatch is undispatchable because the orchestrator cannot ask 'what context informs this unit'. Cascades through DEC-0017 dependencies: FGAP-031 (gather primitive) needs FGAP-029; FGAP-032 (contextBlocks item-level selectivity) needs FGAP-031.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add walk-ancestors(childId, relationType) tool wrapping a new walkAncestors JS export in project-context.ts (symmetric to walkDescendants; reverse-direction DFS over relations edges of the given relation_type, cycle-safe via visited-set). Add walk-by-relation(itemId, relationType, direction: 'in'|'out'|'both') generalized tool covering both directions in one primitive. Item-level scoping per applicability predicate (FGAP-010) optional at first; can layer in later. Cascades into pi-context's project-context.ts SDK + new tool registrations in index.ts.

gaps.title: Per-work-unit-kind context contract substrate missing — no way to declare which relation_types compose the execution bundle for each unit kind
gaps.description: DEC-0017 specifies per-unit-kind context contract: implementation tasks need conventions + acceptance + architecture-touched; decision-making tasks need rationale + alternatives + related-decisions; verification tasks need verification-criteria + evidence-rules + items-under-verification. Today the substrate has NO mechanism to declare per-unit-kind which relation_types compose the execution bundle. gather-execution-context (FGAP-031) cannot dispatch correctly without reading the contract for the unit-kind being executed.

evidence.reference: No context-contract.schema.json; no schema declaring per-kind bundle composition. Existing schemas (config, tasks, phase, decisions, etc.) carry the unit-kind data but no kind-to-bundle mapping.

ConfigBlock declares lenses[] (composition lens machinery from FGAP-012 closure exists); the context-contract could plausibly encode AS composition lenses keyed by unit-kind, OR as a new context-contract block kind. Decision deferred to FGAP-030 closure work.

gaps.impact: Without per-unit-kind context contract, gather-execution-context cannot know what to gather for a given unit. Either gather returns everything (bundle pollution; defeats relevance scoping) or returns nothing (no contract = nothing to bundle). The substrate has no extensibility path for new unit-kinds without code changes — same antipattern as FGAP-013's pre-DEC-0013 hardcoded status vocabulary.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Two coherent implementation shapes — closure work decides which: (a) NEW BLOCK KIND: context-contract.json with schema declaring per-kind { unitKind: string, bundle_relation_types: { in: string[], out: string[], depth: number, applicability_predicate?: string }[] }. Authoring contract per unit-kind is opt-in via /context install (default contracts ship in registry/blocks/). (b) COMPOSITION-LENS ENCODING: context contracts encoded in config.lenses[] as kind='execution-context-bundle' lenses keyed by unit-kind. Reuses FGAP-012 closure machinery. Either is DEC-0017 compliant. (b) is simpler but less explicit; (a) is more explicit but adds a block kind. Closure work picks one + documents rationale.

gaps.title: Composition-of-views work-unit-driven primitive missing — gather-execution-context(unitId, kind) tool and JS export
gaps.description: DEC-0017 specifies a single substrate operation that reads the unit + reads its context contract (FGAP-030) + walks declared relation_types bidirectionally (FGAP-029) + composes the result as one structured bundle returned as one tool return. Today no such primitive exists; orchestrator must do N+1 reads + ad-hoc composition. Under DEC-0014/0016 + FGAP-028 dogfood-completeness, this is the load-bearing primitive between substrate and JIT-agent execution.

evidence.reference: loadLensView composes lens projection but is lens-driven, not work-unit-driven. resolveComposition (FGAP-012 closure) composes member items but doesn't apply per-unit-kind context contract or work-unit-driven traversal. Closest existing surface; insufficient for work-unit bundling.

22-row gap table does not yet enumerate gather-execution-context — DEC-0017 surfaces this as a NEW gap not previously enumerated. Under DEC-0017 it joins as P1 candidate alongside filter-block-items / read-config / read-schema.

gaps.impact: Without gather-execution-context, work-unit dispatch under DEC-0014/0016 requires the orchestrator to: (1) read the unit; (2) read the contract for its kind; (3) walk N relation_types bidirectionally; (4) read each related item; (5) compose the bundle. That's 5+N tool calls per unit dispatch, with hand-rolled composition logic. With the primitive: 1 tool call returns the composed bundle. The N+1 pattern defeats DEC-0014/0016 + FGAP-028 dogfood-dispatchability.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Author gather-execution-context(unitId: string, kind: string): ContextBundle as JS export in packages/pi-context/src/ (likely new module: execution-context.ts, OR within lens-view.ts as work-unit-driven composition). Pi tool wrapper in index.ts: project-gather-execution-context (or context-gather-execution-context post-rename per FGAP-026 Phase 5). Body composes: (1) readBlock(cwd, unit's block) + filter to unitId; (2) read context contract for kind via FGAP-030 mechanism; (3) for each declared relation_type in contract: walkAncestors + walkDescendants (FGAP-029) within depth bound; (4) resolve each reached itemId to full payload via batch resolve (resolve-items-by-id from gap audit); (5) optionally apply applicability predicate (FGAP-010) for relevance filtering; (6) return ContextBundle = { unit, perRelationType: { [rt]: ItemRecord[] }, traversal_depth, scoped_at }.

gaps.title: Item-level contextBlocks selectivity missing in pi-jit-agents — currently injects whole blocks, not work-unit-driven item selections
gaps.description: DEC-0017 specifies dispatch-time injection: agents receive context bundles as composed inputs, not as N whole-block dumps. Today pi-jit-agents' contextBlocks injection mechanism (DEC-0008 typed contextBlocks form) injects WHOLE BLOCKS — agent's compileAgent template gets the entire decisions.json under {{ _decisions }}, not just the decisions reached via gather-execution-context for THIS work-unit. Bundle from FGAP-031 is structured but injection is unstructured-dump.

evidence.reference: compileAgent reads contextBlocks declarations + injects whole block content under {{ _<name> }} variables. No mechanism to inject item-level selections driven by a work-unit's gather-execution-context output. Per the runtime-step-context analysis (analysis/2026-04-15-runtime-step-context.md) this gap was named earlier as 'agent spec must declare contextBlocks with item-level selectivity'.

AgentSpec.contextBlocks current shape per DEC-0008: typed object form { name, item?, focus?, depth? } — item field exists at type level but is per-block-static, not per-dispatch-derived from a work-unit gather.

gaps.impact: Bundle composition (FGAP-031) returns structured per-relation-type item lists, but injection collapses them back to whole-block dumps in the agent's compiled prompt. Agents receive irrelevant context (sibling items not reached via the work-unit's relation walk) + miss work-unit-specific context (the per-relation-type structure). Defeats DEC-0017's selectivity intent.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend AgentSpec.contextBlocks shape to support work-unit-driven gather: { name, gather_for_unit?: { unitId, kind } } — when gather_for_unit is present, compileAgent calls gather-execution-context(unitId, kind) and injects the bundle as { _<name>_bundle: ContextBundle } variable. Templates reference _<block>_bundle.perRelationType.<relation_type>[] for selective rendering. Existing per-item macros (per the macros-as-prompt-substrate work) consume the bundle's per-relation-type collections. Backward compatible: existing { name, item?, focus?, depth? } usage unchanged; gather_for_unit is additive.
